chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.content:
Introduction

Welcome to AXIOM01 v3: The Semantic Rebellion — The Mathematics of Design.

This is not just another design framework. This is a rebellion against the chaos of utility-first CSS and endless class names. This is a return to semantic HTML, element-based styling, and design tokens.

In this guide, you'll learn how to build beautiful, accessible interfaces using pure semantic HTML, clean CSS with design tokens, and minimal JavaScript. No build tools. No complicated tooling. Just HTML, CSS, and JavaScript done right.

The philosophy is simple: one semantic class per component, theming on the semantic elements themselves, and respect for the natural order of the DOM.

chapters.title: The Philosophy
chapters.content:
Chapter 1: The Philosophy

Design systems are built on philosophy. AXIOM01 is built on the principle that semantic HTML is not optional — it's foundational.

One Class Per Component

In BEM or Tailwind, you write: .button__primary--large .button__text. In AXIOM01, you write: .card. That's it. One class. The rest is theming on semantic elements.

Element-Based Styling

Instead of styling classes, we style semantic elements: button { }, h3 { }, a { }. This means the HTML communicates intent, and CSS responds to meaning, not arbitrary class names.

Design Tokens Only

Colors, spacing, typography — all defined as CSS variables. No magic numbers. No hex colors in component CSS. Just var(--a-primary), var(--a-space-md), var(--a-text-base).

chapters.title: Semantic HTML
chapters.content:
Chapter 2: Semantic HTML

Semantic HTML is the foundation. It communicates meaning to both browsers and humans.

The Structure

Every page starts with semantic structure: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <aside>, <footer>.

Why It Matters

Semantic HTML provides:

  • Better accessibility for screen readers
  • Better SEO for search engines
  • Better maintainability for future developers
  • Better styling hooks that don't require class names

chapters.title: CSS Layers & Cascade
chapters.content:
Chapter 3: CSS Layers & Cascade

AXIOM01 uses CSS @layer to organize styles into a predictable cascade.

The Six Layers

  1. reset — Browser defaults normalized
  2. tokens — Design token definitions
  3. base — Element base styles
  4. layout — Layout components (.container, grids)
  5. components — Component styles
  6. utilities — Utility overrides

This layering means specificity wars are over. Everything has its place.

chapters.title: Design Tokens
chapters.content:
Chapter 4: Design Tokens

Design tokens are CSS variables that define the design language. Instead of hardcoding colors, we reference tokens.

Token Categories

Colors: OKLCH color space for perceptual color matching across light and dark modes. var(--a-primary), var(--a-surface-base), var(--a-text-main).

Spacing: Modular scale. var(--a-space-xs) through var(--a-space-4xl).

Typography: Font sizes, weights, line heights. var(--a-text-sm), var(--a-font-weight-medium), var(--a-line-height-relaxed).

Radii: Border radius values. var(--a-radius-sm), var(--a-radius-lg).

chapters.title: Components: Buttons
chapters.content:
Chapter 5: Components — Buttons

Buttons are the simplest component, but they reveal the philosophy clearly.

The Semantic Button

We start with <button>. That's semantic. It tells the browser and assistive technology this is a clickable action.

The CSS

We style the button using button { }. No .btn or .btn--primary. Just button.

For variants (secondary, ghost), we use button.secondary { } or button.ghost { }. One modifier class. That's it.

The Result

Clean HTML: <button>Click</button>. Clean CSS. Maintainable forever.

chapters.title: Components: Cards
chapters.content:
Chapter 6: Components — Cards

Cards are containers. We use semantic HTML for them too.

The Semantic Card

A card is an <article>. It's a self-contained piece of content. CSS: article.card { }.

Structure Inside

<article class='card'> <h3>Title</h3> <p>Content</p> </article>

Heading hierarchy is preserved. No .card__title nonsense.

Variants

article.card-highlighted { } for featured cards. article.card-image { } for cards with images.

chapters.title: Forms & Accessibility
chapters.content:
Chapter 7: Forms & Accessibility

Forms are where semantic HTML and accessibility shine.

Semantic Form Elements

<form>, <fieldset>, <legend>, <label>, <input>, <textarea>, <select>.

These elements have built-in accessibility. A label paired with an input is accessible by default.

ARIA Attributes

For complex interactions, we add ARIA attributes: aria-label, aria-current, aria-expanded, aria-describedby.

These tell assistive technology about state and relationship without adding extra markup.

chapters.title: Responsive Design
chapters.content:
Chapter 8: Responsive Design

Mobile-first. Progressive enhancement. These aren't buzzwords — they're principles.

Mobile First

We start with the mobile layout. Base styles are for mobile. Then we add breakpoints for larger screens.

Breakpoints

320px (mobile), 768px (tablet), 1024px (desktop), 1200px (large desktop).

Fluid Typography

Instead of fixed font sizes at breakpoints, we use CSS clamp() for fluid scaling: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2vw, 2rem).

This means typography scales smoothly from mobile to desktop, no breakpoint jumps.

chapters.title: Dark Mode
chapters.content:
Chapter 9: Dark Mode

Dark mode is not an afterthought. It's built in from day one using OKLCH color space.

Why OKLCH?

OKLCH separates hue, saturation, and lightness in a perceptually uniform space. This means dark mode colors are automatically correct — just adjust the lightness channel.

Implementation

html { --a-primary: oklch(50% 0.3 180); } html[data-theme='dark'] { --a-primary: oklch(70% 0.3 180); }

That's it. One variable adjustment. The color is correct in both modes because OKLCH understands color science.

chapters.title: JavaScript Architecture
chapters.content:
Chapter 10: JavaScript Architecture

We keep JavaScript minimal and focused. The Module Pattern handles everything.

The Module Pattern

Each feature is a self-contained module: Navigation, Config, MobileMenu, HubNav, ComponentSearch, CodeSnippet.

Each module has an init() function called once on DOMContentLoaded. Simple. Predictable.

No Frameworks

No React. No Vue. No build tools. Just vanilla JavaScript with clear responsibilities.

chapters.title: State Management
chapters.content:
Chapter 11: State Management

We manage state with native browser APIs: classList, setAttribute, data attributes.

classList

element.classList.add('hidden'), .remove(), .toggle(). Simple state management.

Attributes

element.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true') for accessibility. data-* for component state.

localStorage

For persistent state (theme preference), we use localStorage. That's it. No database. No API.

chapters.title: Performance
chapters.content:
Chapter 12: Performance

AXIOM01 v3 is built for performance.

Numbers

~2,600 lines of code. ~82 KB uncompressed. ~20-22 KB gzipped. No external dependencies.

Zero Build Tools

No webpack, no rollup, no build process. The code you see is the code that ships.

Native Performance

CSS is parsed and rendered natively. JavaScript is vanilla. No framework overhead. Just browser APIs.

Caching Strategy

ContentLoader uses in-memory caching for JSON content. First load from file, subsequent accesses from cache.

chapters.title: Content Modularization
chapters.content:
Chapter 13: Content Modularization

Content lives in JSON files. Not hardcoded in HTML. Not in a database.

Structure

src/content/components/ — 77 components across 6 JSON files. src/content/chapters/ — 17 chapters and intro/conclusion.

Benefits

Separation of concerns. Content can be authored independently. Easier to update. Easier to extend.

Loading

ContentLoader module handles async loading with caching. No build tools needed.

chapters.title: Scaling AXIOM01
chapters.content:
Chapter 14: Scaling AXIOM01

AXIOM01 grows without complexity.

Adding Components

Add to JSON. Register in JavaScript. Done. No build step.

Theme Customization

Want a different color palette? Change the CSS variables. Automatic theme application across all components.

Multiple Instances

Run multiple instances of AXIOM01. Each with different content. Different themes. Same codebase.

chapters.title: Deployment
chapters.content:
Chapter 15: Deployment

AXIOM01 deploys anywhere.

Static Hosting

GitHub Pages, Netlify, Vercel, S3 — any static host works. No server needed.

Content Updates

Update JSON files in src/content/. Redeploy. That's it.

Version Control

All code in Git. All content in JSON files. Easy to track changes. Easy to roll back.

chapters.title: Case Studies
chapters.content:
Chapter 16: Case Studies

Real-world applications of AXIOM01 principles.

Case Study 1: Design System

A company's design system. 77 components. Organized. Documented. Themeable.

Case Study 2: Documentation Site

Technical documentation. Book-style chapters. Search. Navigation. All working without JavaScript frameworks.

Case Study 3: Product Hub

A product's hub. Multiple sections. Component showcase. Theme switcher. All performant. All accessible.

chapters.title: The Future
chapters.content:
Chapter 17: The Future

AXIOM01 continues to evolve. But the principles don't change.

Next Horizon

More components. More themes. Better tooling for content generation. AI-assisted component documentation.

Community

Open source. MIT license. Contributions welcome. Let's build a community of semantic-first designers and developers.

Thank you for being part of the Semantic Rebellion.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.content:
Conclusion

You've made it to the end of AXIOM01 v3: The Semantic Rebellion — The Mathematics of Design.

You've learned:

  • Why semantic HTML matters
  • How to organize CSS with layers
  • How to use design tokens effectively
  • How to build accessible components
  • How to manage state with native APIs
  • How to scale without complexity

The rebellion is real. It's not just about code. It's about respecting the web. Respecting users. Respecting accessibility. Respecting performance.

Go forth. Build semantic. Build with intention. Build for the web.

This is AXIOM01.
